literal: to be directed, to be ordained/disposed; hence governable, subject to control, manageable — gerundive (kṛtya) of vi-√dhā 'to ordain, arrange, direct' (vi + √dhā + -ya)
invariant_definition: *Vidheya* is the gerundive of *vi-√dhā* ('to ordain, dispose, direct') — literally 'to be directed, to be brought under arrangement', hence **governable, subject to control, manageable**. In the *Bhagavad-Gītā* it appears at 2.64 in the bahuvrīhi *vidheyātmā*, 'he whose self (the inner organ) is governable': the disciplined man who, moving among sense-objects with senses freed from rāga and dveṣa, keeps his mind under his own direction and so attains *prasāda* (serenity). Śaṅkara glosses *vidheya* as the self being *āyatta* — under one's command; it is the positive counterpart of the senses that 'violently carry away the mind' (2.60). *Vidheya* is not docility imposed from without but self-governance: the mind made answerable to one's own discriminating will.

per_school.definition: At 2.64 *vidheyātmā* names the man whose self/mind is governable — held under the direction of the discriminating will — by which, even while engaging objects, he reaches serenity (*prasāda*). The commentators read *vidheya* as *āyatta* / *vaśya*, 'under control'; Rāmānuja restates *vidheyātmā* as *vidheya-manāḥ*, 'whose mind is governable'.

translator_note: Render *vidheya* as 'governable' or 'under control / self-governed' — the gerundive force is 'to be directed'. Avoid 'biddable' or 'docile', which connote servility; *vidheyātmā* is self-mastery, the mind answerable to one's own discriminating will.
